audit_trail.notes: Two full review cycles were completed with three independent reviewers. All reviewers converged on a 'routine' verdict across both cycles. Gemini consistently rated technical soundness higher (8–9) than claude and codex (7), primarily over how much weight to assign to implicit assumptions that are standard in physics practice. The cavity size constraint objection (l < 2/α) was raised by codex in cycle 1 and adopted by claude and gemini in cycle 2. The interaction Hamiltonian metric-factor disagreement (codex vs. claude on would_change_verdict) was not resolved. The paper could not be fully independently verified because the explicit mode functions are in the 2015 paper, which was not included in the review bundle; similarly the Toussaint Comment was not available. HTML rendering dropped the spacetime dimensionality label, which is a known artifact of the arXiv HTML conversion pipeline and does not affect the mathematical content.

general_reader_takeaway: This paper is a short technical reply defending a 2015 result about quantum fields near an accelerating cavity. A critic argued the original calculation was methodologically flawed because it used mathematical tools associated with a region of spacetime causally disconnected from the cavity. The authors show the same result can be derived without those tools, and explain why using them was harmless anyway. Reviewers found the defense technically sound for its narrow purpose, but routine in contribution and limited in broader significance.
human_spotchecks: Verify that Eq. (11) of this reply agrees numerically with Eq. (19) of Lorek-Louko-Dragan, Class. Quant. Grav. 32, 175003 (2015), and that the matrix element definitions in Eqs. (12a)–(12b) are consistent with the explicit Rindler mode functions given in Eqs. (21)–(23) of that paper.

Check whether the Toussaint Comment (CQG 43, 068001, 2026) raises any objections beyond the two-wedge mode issue; the reply addresses only that objection explicitly, and completeness cannot be assessed without reading the Comment.

Confirm the spacetime dimensionality of the calculation: the HTML rendering drops the numeral ('we work in -dimensional Minkowski spacetime'); the original paper [1] should be consulted to confirm whether this is 1+1 or higher-dimensional, and whether the formula agreement holds in that dimension.

Verify that the interaction Hamiltonian in Eq. (6), with flat measure dξ and no explicit metric factor, exactly matches the coupling convention used in the 2015 paper, or that any lapse/volume factor difference is absorbed consistently into the coupling constant.

Check whether the cavity size constraint l < 2/α (required for both walls to lie in the right Rindler wedge) is stated or assumed in the 2015 paper, and whether its omission here is an oversight or a deliberate reliance on that paper's setup.

Assess whether the continuum Omega integrals in Eq. (11) are well-defined under the intended regularization, in particular the behavior near Omega = 0 (infrared/near-horizon regime) with the specific Rindler mode functions of the 2015 paper.
public_summary: This 3-page reply by Lorek, Louko, and Dragan responds to a Comment by V. Toussaint (CQG 43, 068001, 2026) questioning a de-excitation probability formula in their 2015 paper on ideal clocks and accelerated cavities. The Comment objected that the original derivation invoked Rindler modes from both the cavity's Rindler wedge and the causally disconnected opposing wedge. The reply has two parts: (1) a re-derivation of the de-excitation formula (Eq. 11) working entirely within the right Rindler wedge, using the Unruh thermal state as the ambient field's initial condition; and (2) a causality argument explaining why the original two-wedge intermediate steps were legitimate. All three reviewers across two cycles find the argument structurally sound and the stated purpose achieved. Several implicit assumptions — notably a cavity size constraint required for the geometry to be well-defined, and reliance on mode functions from the 2015 paper for the central formula — prevent the reply from being a fully self-contained verification. The paper is appropriately scoped as a formal reply: it does not introduce new physics but provides a cleaner derivation path and a physical argument that directly addresses the stated objection.
reviewer_disagreements: Gemini (cycle 1) lists no missing assumptions and no technical objections, rating technical soundness at 9/10; claude and codex identify several implicit assumptions (cavity size constraint, formal nature of continuum thermal state, mode function deference) and rate technical soundness at 7/10. Gemini partially updated in cycle 2 but remains more charitable.

Codex marks the interaction Hamiltonian metric-factor objection as would_change_verdict_if_true: true (confidence 0.58–0.63); claude disagrees, arguing λ can trivially absorb any such factor and the would_change_verdict flag should be false. No resolution was reached.

Gemini treats the missing spacetime dimensionality as purely an HTML rendering artifact not attributable to the paper; claude flags it as an ambiguity in the submitted record worth noting, though both agree it does not affect the mathematical content.

strongest_objections.objection: The cavity length constraint l < 2/α, required for both cavity walls to lie strictly within the right Rindler wedge, is never stated. Without this bound the left cavity wall is at or beyond the Rindler horizon and the geometric setup, Dirichlet boundary conditions, and mode expansion are undefined.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section II, cavity endpoint definitions before Eq. (3)

strongest_objections.objection: The explicit mode functions u_n and v_k needed to evaluate the matrix elements I_{n,k} and J_{n,k} in Eqs. (12a)–(12b) are deferred to Eqs. (21)–(23) of the 2015 paper without reproduction. The core integrals that produce the de-excitation rate are therefore not independently verifiable from this reply alone; the agreement claim between Eq. (11) and Eq. (19) of the original paper rests on an external check.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section V, Eqs. (12a)–(12b) and the sentence after Eq. (13)

strongest_objections.objection: The thermal density matrix in Eq. (5) is written over continuum Rindler modes and used under a trace in Section V as if it were an ordinary trace-class density matrix. In rigorous terms this object is distributional; a controlled calculation requires box normalization, wave-packet regularization, or a KMS algebraic-state formulation.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section III Eq. (5), Section V Eqs. (10)–(11)

strongest_objections.objection: The perturbative evolution is taken from η = −∞ to η = +∞ without specifying an adiabatic switching prescription, a regulator, or a convergence argument. For continuum QFT the resulting transition probability integrals can be distributional or conditionally convergent without such a prescription.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section IV Eqs. (7)–(8), Section V Eq. (11)

strongest_objections.objection: The interaction Hamiltonian in Eq. (6) uses the flat measure dξ without stating whether metric lapse or volume factors are absorbed into the coupling constant λ or intentionally omitted. For a covariant local interaction density in the Rindler metric a factor would appear, which could alter the overlap integrals γ and potentially break the claimed exact agreement with the 2015 formula.
strongest_objections.paper_location: Section IV, Eq. (6), and matrix element definitions in Eq. (12)

strongest_supported_claims: The right Rindler wedge is a globally hyperbolic spacetime, and hypersurfaces of constant Rindler time provide a valid Cauchy foliation that can be used to evolve the coupled cavity-ambient system (Sections I, IV).

The ambient field state induced in the right Rindler wedge by the Minkowski vacuum is the standard Unruh thermal state given in Eq. (5), a well-established result cited to Birrell and Davies (Section III).

The leading contribution to the cavity de-excitation probability is at second order in the coupling λ, because the cavity starts in the first excited state and the first-order perturbative term contributes zero to the relevant trace (Section V, after Eq. 10).

Causality is enforced by the spatial support of the interaction Hamiltonian, not by the spatial support of the basis modes used in intermediate steps; using globally supported modes such as Minkowski plane waves or Rindler modes from both wedges does not by itself imply acausal dynamics (Section VI).
technical_takeaway: The paper aims to re-derive the O(λ²) cavity de-excitation probability of Lorek-Louko-Dragan (2015) via first-order perturbation theory restricted entirely to the right Rindler wedge, with the ambient field initialized in the Unruh thermal state (the Minkowski vacuum reduced to the right wedge). The derivation exploits global hyperbolicity of the right Rindler wedge with its constant-η Cauchy foliation. The causality argument asserts that since the interaction Hamiltonian is supported only within the cavity, physical transition probabilities are independent of the spatial extent of the basis modes used in intermediate steps — by analogy with inertial cavities where Minkowski plane waves extend over all of spacetime without violating causality. The paper claims Eq. (11) agrees with Eq. (19) of the 2015 paper; this equality is structurally plausible but cannot be independently verified from the reply alone, as the explicit mode functions are deferred to the original paper.
